sentences.text: Well, in the ghetto, there were lot of people working, because anybody who doesn't work will be, you know.
So they took them to snow, to the movers, lot of people for the German, like a watcher, like a shoemaker, let me say, like, you know, carpet and electrician.
They need a lot of tradesmen.
And a lot of-- and there, we work for the SS commander too, you know.
Like a train came, we were unloading uniiform for the Germans.
So we went-- you know, any job they have, shovel, to make shovel or anything, picks, anything what they have.
But that time already, in Krakow-Plaszow, in the Jewish cemetery, now Krakow-Plaszow, Jewish cemetery, there was already ghetto.
There was a concentration camp, Arbeitslager.
They were working for the Wehrmacht, or for the Nazi.
They're working all the brushes, picks, shovels.
They were anything what they need, anything what they need.
The SS took the contract, and the Jewish people end at Krakow-Plaszow cemetery.
There was an Arbeitslager.
People worked there.
They worked 12 hours a day in any-- there was a [NON-ENGLISH].
There was for the keys, for the locks, anything, because there was a lot of craftsmen, Jewish people, lot of them.
So we were in that.
We were in it.
So they were already-- they get the concentration camp established.
About 19, they cut me once more, once where they cut me to Auschwitz.
And once when I escaped by myself.
Then nobody went from the train, just me.
I cut wire.
It was hard for me because to cut the wire is so hard.
It took me 20 minutes to cut just one wire.
I didn't have enough strength to cut through.
So you're supposed to go around, around, around, around, I remember, and then break it off then.
And put it straight, and then I escaped twice.
In 1943, my mother was working for the Wehrmacht uniform, and my sister.
And I didn't work.
There was an A and B Arbeits, and that people who didn't work.
And they put a fence, right through a fence.
But I didn't care, because my mother-- and one night, they came in, and they blocked us off.
So we couldn't enter to the A.
You know, there was a, there was a ghetto.
But they cut the ghetto in half.
People who didn't work, they were standing there.
SS was right on the whole line watching us.
And people who work.
So all the Wehrmacht, all the people, all the company, the Wehrmacht, or the OT, or the Fliers, who need people to work, they came to the gate.
And they took the people out to work.
From A.
And the B--
I wanted B.
And then I remember my father's word.
Don't ever volunteer first.
Always be the last.
Remember, they're killing the other people.
They need people to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember when they came in with the truck.
They broke the fence.
They brought a lot of trucks.
And there was a lot of children, a lot of children, maybe about five, six, around the children age-- 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10.
So we helped them.
Without even telling Germany, we helped them.
I helped them to put the children on the truck because we wanted they should take us to work.
I didn't want to go to Auschwitz, because the truck fill up, fill up the-- and you have 50 people.
And behind them was a bi-van, a motorcycle with a machine gun, so nobody could escape all the way to Auschwitz, about 100 kilometer.
Took us about two hour.
So nobody could escape.
So I knew it, when I'm in Auschwitz, I would have a hard time.
So I want to be in a ghetto to find something to help them, you know, like be active.
But when there was maybe about 60, 70 people, or even 100 people-- I don't know how much was there.
So Goeth said in German to us, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
We need 60 people to work.
So now, tell me where you work.
So I show him my pass, what he sign it.
What he gave me pass, so we worked for the, for him.
So he said, OK, go to your left.
And the rest people, they took to a corner there.
And they knew that they would shoot them, so they run to the buildings.
A lot of them made it to the buildings.
A lot of them fell dead, you know, right on the spot, you know.
With machine gun, three guys with machine gun, they can kill a lot of.
But a lot of escape.
I would say 50, 50 escaped.
Into the building, in  the ghetto.
In a building.
The building was maybe about 50 feet   for us.
You know, they were around-- but you know, like I said, if you run to a building, you can wind up 10 buildings away.
Because you jump to that, or you go to the attic, or you go on the roof.
You can-- you know.
Oh, maybe about a quarter of a mile from the place when, when the Goeth took us, took us.
Put me away.
No, this is in the ghetto.
No, we couldn't go out.
This is with the, not the Arbeits, the B, the B section.
OK.
And we crossed to the A section because they broke the wire already.
We crossed through.
We went to the building.
And when we walk up, we heard baby crying, crying, screaming.
And smell.
When we went up, we saw hundreds of babies of the floor.
And he told us to this, I want you to take two babies out and put it on the sidewalk, right by the house.
Put them separate.
So I said this is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I said, this is impossible.
He said, you dirty Jew.
You dirty Jew, you say something, I will kill you, and a gun right to my temple.
But I didn't say nothing because I was more concerned about that looking on a dead body, you know, like somebody, you know.
But what we found out later, that we will take the head from that side, and the head from that side.
So we can have it both head exposed later, both head exposed, or it was a little hard.
Supposed to go that just the head should be exposed from both baby, not the head together.
Carrying baby down, 3/4 finished.
There was about 400 babies there, 300, 400.
Carried the baby down and put them right on the sidewalk, like the sidewalk, like this, sidewalk all the way lying this way, lying this way, lying this way, all the way down.
And the building was over 100 feet long.
Everything was occupied.
He took a gun out.
And he shot each baby in his head, killed them.
Loaded magazine and killed them.
Loaded and killed them.
And we saw it.
And we stopped and screamed.
But he didn't kill us because he knows if he kill me, he kill him, he's supposed to do the job himself.
So he didn't want to kill nobody.
He know that we're supposed to-- we are the slaves.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And we finished that.
And I saw the baby just slaughtered without mercy, without nothing.
This is in my mind.
I can never forget it how long I live.
And it bothers me.
And it's-- you know, I have grandchildren.
I'm a father, you know.
And I told myself if there would be mafia, if that would be mafia, they would have respect for something, for somebody, especially for children, innocent children.
But the butchers, they killed us.
They're murderers.
The pillow was on the top.
So he kicked the pillow out, and he killed the baby.
Face was down, so he shot the baby.
And I mean, and we were standing there.
Then he told us to-- there came a platform, a horse and buggy, of maybe about 12 feet, 12 by 7 by 8.
And the arms, so he can lift the arms higher, or he can lift the arms lower.
So that the U shape was closed.
The front to throw the baby, to throw the baby, and the platform was open, was down.
That means all day, one, two, three-- three, what do you call them-- three wings, so he can lower down or lift it up and hook this together.
And after we were through, fill up all the baby, another horse and buggy came in, you know, there.
And we were completely with blood, completely.
We were supposed to take our hand, only thing what is left over, you know, that we shouldn't put out with the dum dum bullets, you know, this is explosion something.
Because we saw like you're cutting all of them in half.
And sometimes he shot twice, you know, that we're supposed to take it and clean everything up, so that it would be clean.
But when I saw the horse and buggy moved away, I saw the blood running from every side.
The blood was running out.
You can see like a road, like in snow, somebody plowed in the middle of forest.
Somebody took a tractor and cleaned the whole thing.
And you can see their blood all the way.
And we were stood.
And after we're through with this, he took us to the old folks' home, oh, maybe another 100 feet.
Tell me who's upstairs.
We didn't want to say nothing no more.
We were stupid.
We didn't say nothing.
So we were walking upstairs, and he saw the people in the bed, 60, 70, 75, 80, you know.
So he shot them right in the bed, killed them with a machine gun, killed them.
There were maybe about 300 of them, 200.
A horse and buggy came in again.
We took the chair, he told us, and break the frame from the window.
[SIGHS] We broke the frame from the window, and we tossed the body down on the truck, on the platform for the buggy.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After we're through with that, we didn't know what to say.
We were like a robot.
Push the button, and we do it.
Went to the hospital, and there were a lot of German there in hospital.
They start to shooting the people in the bed, in the bed, in the bed.
He was in the hospital.
He was there.
He was-- he never wore a uniform.
I never saw him in uniform, only in [INAUDIBLE] clothes.
And my friend plays soccer, and he's-- he broke his leg about two, three weeks ago.
Well, I came to the hospital, so the German shoot him, shot him right in the head.
But we supposed to take the body because he was like this, foot high, you know, in the bed.
But a lot of times, we missed the platform.
Because the-- There was too many people there.
So when the body hit the platform, he was like-- like a dead chicken you throw down and hit the sidewalk.
Blood was running this.
We were completely in blood.
So now, we asked him where we can change the clothes there.
So we took from clothes from the people who were dead, you know, because we were completely soaked with blood, everything.
In one day, one day.
We marched to the-- to Krakow-Plaszow, Arbeitslager, concentration camp, Arbeitslager, the same day, night time.
Figure March 12, I think, so, or March 13, 1943.
So we walked maybe about 3 kilometer, 3 or 4 kilometer, we march.
By the time we got 90 people already, 90 people.
When the Goeth-- well, we saw Goeth in the ghetto.
I saw Goeth maybe 20 times, because he was there left and right.
He got the men.
He took about four, five people of them, Ukraine, and went to the building looking for the-- who was hiding in it, in a basement.
Like I said, in basement, in attic, and you know, because people didn't want to go to the concentration camp.
A lot escape to the wall.
When I took us, and I remember that when I took us to the camp, we didn't know the law.
When a German coming into the barracks, everybody was supposed to stay salute.
Like a captain coming to a barrack, or major.
[INAUDIBLE].
So we didn't know.
Goeth came to the barrack.
There was no kapo.
There was no Arbeiter for us, the guy who knows the law.
And we didn't know what's going on.
We were minding our own business.
And when the German came in, especially Goeth.
But he said like this.
Today, we spilt plenty blood.
Enough blood was spilled today.
I will forgive you for this.
Because we're supposed to stay attention when he came to the barracks.
And because I choose you, so I forgive you.
Because he could take us and just shoot us, and nobody would say nothing.
And then right away, the police came in and established a kapo, told them what to do, what is it, what's supposed to be done when the German come in to the barrack.
They were supposed to jump from the bed up and stay attention.
So--
First night in the concentration camp Plaszow, Krakow-Plaszow.
Same day.
Same day.
It was night time.
The same day.
I march.
We came to give us a barrack.
And Goeth want to see what kind of people he choose, because he choose all of us to survive, to work for them.
So we didn't know the law, that w'ere supposed to stay attention that.
So he forgive us for this.
And then the Jewish officer, because there were Jewish officer there, policeman there, told us what is the law.
And they right away took a kapo.
That meant [NON-ENGLISH], the guy who knows.
He would tell-- let me say German came in, I need five people.
So they didn't come to me.
They come to him.
And he get five people to work.
Or you work there, or you work there.
They needed somebody who knows.
Let me say, the Jewish said, listen.
I need 50 people.
Command the five.
You, you go down there.
So they have a-- they call it kapo [NON-ENGLISH].
In the Krakow-Plaszow concentration camp.
My mother was there.
And after I came in, I still was to wash myself.
There was a latrine there.
I went in.
I washed myself really, truly with cold water.
Then I see my mother, you know, and my sister.
And my, my mother thought that I wouldn't be here, but I came.
Yeah.
Yeah, you could walk in.
In the ghetto-- in the concentration camp, you could walk in.
There was a section, woman's section, a man's section.
So we went to the woman's section there, and I saw my mother.
My brother, my brother went to Auschwitz.
My brother went to Auschwitz.
One brother was in Russia.
And my sister was in camp.
And I was in camp.
Auschwitz.
He was sent to Auschwitz.
They took him to Auschwitz.
In 1942, in June.
And later, three months later, they took my brother to Auschwitz.
And I never saw him again.
So after, I got a job in a Steinbruch.
What the job was there?
Another person with myself, we're supposed to drill a hole in the mountain.
Steinbruch is-- Steinbruch, that means when you drill a hole in a mountain.
You blow the mountain, so you can have a gravel for the road.
How would you put it in?
Oh, we dynamite the mountain.
Yeah, we dynamite the mountain.
Krakow-Plaszow.
The next day, the next four or five days, I got the job.
And we drill holes.
I saw Goeth.
I saw Schindler coming three, four times a week, drinking, radio playing.
Excuse me.
Not far away, we drilled the holes, there was at Hitler's-- Goeth house, 200, 300 feet.
A lot of times Goeth went in, out from his house, work on the mountain there.
And we drilled steps.
We drilled that.
Then we drilled higher, then a little higher, because we couldn't go all the time.
We made like steps.
Let my friend drill, I helped him, or I stood, you know, and watched.
Because what we do, we make myself a platform.
And I put the compressor, and the compressor hammer we put on the platform.
Only thing we were supposed to do, press it against the rock.
You know, press it, release Press it, release.
So we didn't hold it.
We didn't hold it in our hand, because it was sitting on a platform from wood.
It was easy.
And that's when we found out that we supposed to watch him.
We're supposed to watch the Goeth when he's coming from his house, all around the mountain there, and to us.
And any time there was explosion, he liked to know about it.
He likes to know about it.
But once, I have occasioned proclamation was in Krakow that anybody who find a Jew hidden any place, we will give you kilo sugar, a kilo sugar to the Polish people.
So the Polish people tried to look for the Jews who were hidden, forest, outside the forest by the Gentile.
It's no difference where.
And when they caught 30, 40 Jewish children, parents, brought to Krakow-Plaszow ghetto.
And they brought to the special place.
There was a big mountain and a big hole in it.
So a lot of times, they took our commander from the Steinbruch.
There was three, four people they took.
And we supposed-- we spray special chemicals so there would not be typhus.
And they were covered with a foot of dirt.
We walk on the body.
We walk on the body.
Cover it with a foot of dirt.
We always put the dirt on the side.
So with the shovel, we throw inside, from all around.
And then-- and there was the next people came in, we do the same thing all over.
But I remember one thing now.
They came in about 50 Polish underground.
So we were very close.
We work here, and the thing was, the whole thing was maybe about 100 feet away.
So they were tied in completely, like you tied it in, completely tied in.
They couldn't walk.
We supposed to carry them from the truck.
And they put a log this way, six, seven log in that Polish underground, this way.
A log and this way.
We pile up maybe about 6, 7 feet.
And there were three or four or five of bunches, you know, of the logs.
German went in and put gas on the top.
He took a gun and ignite it.
And there was screaming.
And there was singing, like we sing, 'Oh Beautiful' here, you know.
They were singing [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know.
Screaming, to see this, it's unbelievable thing, human being were burned alive, alive.
And then, after thing what happened, there was nothing.
There was two, three wheelbarrows.
We took the wheelbarrows, throw to the mountain there, throw it like this because there was ashes.
The wood was burning for two days.
Smell, that smell is so bad.
It's unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I do remember this.
I didn't mention that 1944, we were marching.
And the Russian told us to sing [NON-ENGLISH].
This is the Russian song.
This is forbidden to sing.
But they were Russian.
But they were watching us.
They were [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
So after we came to our camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one guard, who was watching us, he said, he sing that song, young boy.
And I do remember what Goeth done.
He told the Jewish people that if ever he catch a Jew with a beard, he will kill him.
A beard, they will kill them.
So what Orthodox Jew, they didn't have nothing.
They took the pride away, so the only thing what they had is the beard.
But for me, you can-- beard, you can grow later a beard.
When he took the handkerchief off, there was a beard.
So he killed them.
So he killed.
He took machine gun on a horse, and he killed.
He was just a vicious animal, most vicious animal that this-- that-- I can't understand it.
He killed.
He killed.
He killed.
